long time are prepared to let others do so too while waiting for a start time.

QOSL is implemented by two SLURM QoS definitions: icelake-long and cclake-long have `cpul`, which is restricted to 512 cpus per user; ampere-long has `gpul`, which is restricted to 32 GPUs per user.

In order to apply for access to QOSL, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk detailing why this mode of usage is necessary, and explaining why checkpointing is not a practical option for your application.

## Submitting long jobs[¶](#submitting-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Use of QOSL is tied to the -long partitions, therefore once given access it is necessary only to specify this partition - e.g.

```
sbatch -t 7-0:0:0 -p icelake-long -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
```

[TICKET_SUMMARY] SLURM: Concerning Queue Time

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
My name is Jacob, and I occasionally submit SLURM jobs to the Icelake CPU cluster.
Roughly 5 days ago, I submitted ~20 jobs to Icelake, each requesting 1 node, 16 CPUs, and a maximum time limit of 12 hours.  It's been 5 days, and my jobs still haven't run; they're sitting indefinitely in the queue:
[cid:d3e486ac-2b91-4104-bf38-8d7f36d648ab]
I've submitted similar jobs before, and I never had jobs stuck in the queue this long. These particular jobs are for an upcoming, time-sensitive deadline.
Thus, I wanted to ask you:
1.
Is there any way you can prioritise these jobs so they're run and I can meet my deadline?
2.
Is there any way I can estimate queue times before I submit future jobs?
I apologise for sending this enquiry, as I know this long queue time was unintentional. However, I hope there's something you can do to allow these highly time-sensitive jobs to be run...
Jacob

# [Slurm Workload Manager](/)

[SchedMD](https://www.schedmd.com/)

# Job Reason Codes

These reason codes can be used to identify why a pending job has not yet been
started by the scheduler. There may be multiple reasons why a job cannot start,
in which case only the reason that was encountered by the attempted scheduling
method will be displayed. Refer to the [Scheduling Configuration Guide](sched_config.html) for more details.

## Common Reasons

* **AssocGrp\*** — The job's association has reached an aggregate
  [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **AssocMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a maximum
  [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for the
  requested association.
* **BeginTime** — The job's earliest [start time](sbatch.html#OPT_begin) has not yet been reached.
* **Dependency** — This job has a
  [dependency](sbatch.html#OPT_dependency) on another job that has not
  been satisfied.
* **Max\*PerAccount** — A portion of the job request exceeds the
  per-Account [limit](resource_limits.html) on the job's
  [QOS](qos.html).
* **Priority** — One of more higher
  [priority](priority_multifactor.html) jobs exist for the partition
  associated with the job or for the [advanced
  reservation](reservations.html).
* **QOSGrp\*** — The job's [QOS](qos.html) has reached an
  aggregate [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **QOSMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a
  maximum [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for
  the requested [QOS](qos.html).
* **Resources** — The resources requested by the job are not
  available (e.g., already used by other jobs).

## All Reasons

* **AccountingPolicy** — Fallback reason when others not matched.
* **AccountNotAllowed** — Job is in an account not allowed in a
  partition.
* **AssocGrpBB** — The job's association has reached its aggregate
  Burst Buffer limit.
* **AssocGrpBBMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by past,
  present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBBRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by
  currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpBilling** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Billing limit.
* **AssocGrpBillingMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of
  a resource by past, present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBillingRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of a
  resource by currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpCpuLimit** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate CPU limit.
* **AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by past, present
  and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpCPURunMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by currently
  running jobs.
* **AssocGrpEnergy** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Energy limit.
* **AssocGrpEnergyMinutes** — The

lowest quality of service in operation on the cluster. This is the default service level for users who are not eligible for higher service levels.

SL4 jobs only run when there are no other eligible jobs in the queue. Users relying on SL4 to run a job should expect long wait times.

SL4 is designed to make use of unused compute cycles by allowing SL1, SL2 and SL3 users who have reached their limits to make use of unutilised compute cycles.

## Quality of Service[¶](#quality-of-service "Permalink to this headline")

**QOS1 – highest quality of service**

* QOS1 jobs have the highest priority and will move through the queue fastest.
* QOS1 jobs have a maximum job run time of 36 hours.
* QOS1 jobs have a maximum per-job limit on the number of resource units that a single job can take:
* Peta4 (CPU) jobs have a 4256 CPU core limit
* Wilkes3 (GPU) jobs have a 64 GPU limit.

The same limits apply, on each cluster separately, to QOS1 jobs overall belonging to the same user.

**QOS2 – medium quality of service**

* QOS2 jobs have a lower priority than QOS1 and will move through the queue more slowly than QOS1 jobs.
* QOS2 jobs have a maximum job run time of 12 hours.
* QOS2 jobs have a maximum per-job limit on the number of resource units that a single job can take:
* Peta4 (CPU) jobs have a 448 CPU core limit
* Wilkes3 (GPU) jobs have a 32 GPU limit.

The same limits apply, on each cluster separately, to QOS2 jobs overall belonging to the same user.

**QOS3 – lowest quality of service**

* QOS3 jobs have the lowest priority and jobs will only run when there are no eligible QOS1

# [Slurm Workload Manager](/)

[SchedMD](https://www.schedmd.com/)

# Slurm Power Saving Guide

## Contents

## Overview

Slurm provides an integrated mechanism for nodes being suspended (powered
down, placed into power saving mode) and resumed (powered up, restored from
power saving mode) on demand or by request. Nodes that remain *IDLE* for
**SuspendTime** will be suspended by **SuspendProgram** and will be
unavailable for scheduling for **SuspendTimeout**. Nodes will
automatically be resumed by **ResumeProgram** to complete work allocated
to them. Nodes that fail to register within **ResumeTimeout** will become
*DOWN* and their allocated jobs are requeued. Node power saving can be
manually requested by `scontrol update nodename=<nodename>
state=power_<down|up>`. The rate of nodes being resumed or
suspended can be controlled by **ResumeRate** and **SuspendRate**.

Slurm can be configured to accomplish power saving by managing compute
resources in any cloud provider (e.g. [Amazon
Web Services](http://aws.amazon.com/), [Google Cloud
Platform](https://cloud.google.com/), [Microsoft Azure](https://azure.microsoft.com/)) via
their API. These resources can be combined with an existing cluster to
process excess workload (cloud bursting) or it can operate as an independent
and self-contained cluster.

To enable Power Saving operation in Slurm, you must configure the
following:

* **ResumeProgram** and **SuspendProgram** must be defined. Their
  value must be a valid path to a program.
* **ResumeTimeout** and **SuspendTimeout** must be defined, either
  globally or on at least one partition.
* **SuspendTime** must be defined, either globally or on at least one
  partition, and not be *INFINITE* or *-1*.
* **ResumeRate** and **SuspendRate** must be greater than or equal
  to *0*.

The Slurm control daemon, *slurmctld*, must be restarted to initially
enable Power Saving operation. Changes in the configuration parameters (e.g.
**SuspendTime**) will take effect after modifying the *slurm.conf*
configuration file and executing `scontrol reconfigure`.

## Configuration

The following configuration parameters of interest include:

**DebugFlags**
:   Defines specific subsystems which should provide more detailed event
    logging. Options of interest include:

    **Power**
    :   Power management plugin and power save (suspend/resume programs)
        details.

**ReconfigFlags**
:   Flags to control various actions that may be taken when an
    `scontrol reconfigure` command is issued. Options of interest
    include:

    **KeepPowerSaveSettings**
    :   If set, an `scontrol reconfigure` command will preserve
        the current state of **SuspendExcNodes**, **SuspendExcParts**,
        and **SuspendExcStates**.

**ResumeFailProgram**
:   Program to be executed when nodes fail to resume by
    **ResumeTimeout**. The argument to the program will be the names of
    the failed nodes (using Slurm's hostlist expression format).

**ResumeProgram**
:   Program to be executed to restore nodes from power saving mode. The
    program executes as *SlurmUser* (as configured in
    *slurm.conf*). The argument to the program will be the names of
    nodes to be

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Job submission

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
Can you share a job submission script so I can have a look at the resources you are requesting. During the cluster maintenance in July we decommissioned a large part of the cluster which might have caused longer queues times for the remaining partitions. We are in the process of adding more compute capacity in September and October which will create a new partition and hopefully reduce the wait times for jobs in the queue.
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Hi,
I ran someone else’s batch file and it ran instantly.
I noticed I had written the following in my batch script, I removed it and it seems to be working a lot better today. I am wondering if this requests a specific node when added to a script like this.

#SBATCH -c 1
Thanks,
Steve

</MESSAGE><MESSAGE id=0003 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Steve,
This option restricts the amount of CPUs per task. This probably conflicted with the other directives in your submissions script and caused your job to remain in a pending state. I’ll close this ticket now since you managed to resolve it. Let us know if you have any other questions or issues by opening a new ticket with [support@hpc.cam.ac.uk](mailto:support@hpc.cam.ac.uk)
Kind regards,
Avraam

</MESSAGE>

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

## Problems[¶](#problems "Permalink to this headline")

Please check first of all whether there is an answer to your question in the FAQ. If not, please request support with your details and

QOSL is implemented by two SLURM QoS definitions: icelake-long and cclake-long have `cpul`, which is restricted to 512 cpus per user; ampere-long has `gpul`, which is restricted to 32 GPUs per user.

In order to apply for access to QOSL, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk detailing why this mode of usage is necessary, and explaining why checkpointing is not a practical option for your application.

## Submitting long jobs[¶](#submitting-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Use of QOSL is tied to the -long partitions, therefore once given access it is necessary only to specify this partition - e.g.

```
sbatch -t 7-0:0:0 -p icelake-long -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
```

# [Slurm Workload Manager](/)

[SchedMD](https://www.schedmd.com/)

# Job Reason Codes

These reason codes can be used to identify why a pending job has not yet been
started by the scheduler. There may be multiple reasons why a job cannot start,
in which case only the reason that was encountered by the attempted scheduling
method will be displayed. Refer to the [Scheduling Configuration Guide](sched_config.html) for more details.

## Common Reasons

* **AssocGrp\*** — The job's association has reached an aggregate
  [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **AssocMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a maximum
  [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for the
  requested association.
* **BeginTime** — The job's earliest [start time](sbatch.html#OPT_begin) has not yet been reached.
* **Dependency** — This job has a
  [dependency](sbatch.html#OPT_dependency) on another job that has not
  been satisfied.
* **Max\*PerAccount** — A portion of the job request exceeds the
  per-Account [limit](resource_limits.html) on the job's
  [QOS](qos.html).
* **Priority** — One of more higher
  [priority](priority_multifactor.html) jobs exist for the partition
  associated with the job or for the [advanced
  reservation](reservations.html).
* **QOSGrp\*** — The job's [QOS](qos.html) has reached an
  aggregate [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **QOSMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a
  maximum [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for
  the requested [QOS](qos.html).
* **Resources** — The resources requested by the job are not
  available (e.g., already used by other jobs).

## All Reasons

* **AccountingPolicy** — Fallback reason when others not matched.
* **AccountNotAllowed** — Job is in an account not allowed in a
  partition.
* **AssocGrpBB** — The job's association has reached its aggregate
  Burst Buffer limit.
* **AssocGrpBBMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by past,
  present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBBRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by
  currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpBilling** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Billing limit.
* **AssocGrpBillingMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of
  a resource by past, present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBillingRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of a
  resource by currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpCpuLimit** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate CPU limit.
* **AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by past, present
  and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpCPURunMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by currently
  running jobs.
* **AssocGrpEnergy** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Energy limit.
* **AssocGrpEnergyMinutes** — The

lowest quality of service in operation on the cluster. This is the default service level for users who are not eligible for higher service levels.

SL4 jobs only run when there are no other eligible jobs in the queue. Users relying on SL4 to run a job should expect long wait times.

SL4 is designed to make use of unused compute cycles by allowing SL1, SL2 and SL3 users who have reached their limits to make use of unutilised compute cycles.

## Quality of Service[¶](#quality-of-service "Permalink to this headline")

**QOS1 – highest quality of service**

* QOS1 jobs have the highest priority and will move through the queue fastest.
* QOS1 jobs have a maximum job run time of 36 hours.
* QOS1 jobs have a maximum per-job limit on the number of resource units that a single job can take:
* Peta4 (CPU) jobs have a 4256 CPU core limit
* Wilkes3 (GPU) jobs have a 64 GPU limit.

The same limits apply, on each cluster separately, to QOS1 jobs overall belonging to the same user.

**QOS2 – medium quality of service**

* QOS2 jobs have a lower priority than QOS1 and will move through the queue more slowly than QOS1 jobs.
* QOS2 jobs have a maximum job run time of 12 hours.
* QOS2 jobs have a maximum per-job limit on the number of resource units that a single job can take:
* Peta4 (CPU) jobs have a 448 CPU core limit
* Wilkes3 (GPU) jobs have a 32 GPU limit.

The same limits apply, on each cluster separately, to QOS2 jobs overall belonging to the same user.

**QOS3 – lowest quality of service**

* QOS3 jobs have the lowest priority and jobs will only run when there are no eligible QOS1

# [Slurm Workload Manager](/)

[SchedMD](https://www.schedmd.com/)

# Slurm Power Saving Guide

## Contents

## Overview

Slurm provides an integrated mechanism for nodes being suspended (powered
down, placed into power saving mode) and resumed (powered up, restored from
power saving mode) on demand or by request. Nodes that remain *IDLE* for
**SuspendTime** will be suspended by **SuspendProgram** and will be
unavailable for scheduling for **SuspendTimeout**. Nodes will
automatically be resumed by **ResumeProgram** to complete work allocated
to them. Nodes that fail to register within **ResumeTimeout** will become
*DOWN* and their allocated jobs are requeued. Node power saving can be
manually requested by `scontrol update nodename=<nodename>
state=power_<down|up>`. The rate of nodes being resumed or
suspended can be controlled by **ResumeRate** and **SuspendRate**.

Slurm can be configured to accomplish power saving by managing compute
resources in any cloud provider (e.g. [Amazon
Web Services](http://aws.amazon.com/), [Google Cloud
Platform](https://cloud.google.com/), [Microsoft Azure](https://azure.microsoft.com/)) via
their API. These resources can be combined with an existing cluster to
process excess workload (cloud bursting) or it can operate as an independent
and self-contained cluster.

To enable Power Saving operation in Slurm, you must configure the
following:

* **ResumeProgram** and **SuspendProgram** must be defined. Their
  value must be a valid path to a program.
* **ResumeTimeout** and **SuspendTimeout** must be defined, either
  globally or on at least one partition.
* **SuspendTime** must be defined, either globally or on at least one
  partition, and not be *INFINITE* or *-1*.
* **ResumeRate** and **SuspendRate** must be greater than or equal
  to *0*.

The Slurm control daemon, *slurmctld*, must be restarted to initially
enable Power Saving operation. Changes in the configuration parameters (e.g.
**SuspendTime**) will take effect after modifying the *slurm.conf*
configuration file and executing `scontrol reconfigure`.

## Configuration

The following configuration parameters of interest include:

**DebugFlags**
:   Defines specific subsystems which should provide more detailed event
    logging. Options of interest include:

    **Power**
    :   Power management plugin and power save (suspend/resume programs)
        details.

**ReconfigFlags**
:   Flags to control various actions that may be taken when an
    `scontrol reconfigure` command is issued. Options of interest
    include:

    **KeepPowerSaveSettings**
    :   If set, an `scontrol reconfigure` command will preserve
        the current state of **SuspendExcNodes**, **SuspendExcParts**,
        and **SuspendExcStates**.

**ResumeFailProgram**
:   Program to be executed when nodes fail to resume by
    **ResumeTimeout**. The argument to the program will be the names of
    the failed nodes (using Slurm's hostlist expression format).

**ResumeProgram**
:   Program to be executed to restore nodes from power saving mode. The
    program executes as *SlurmUser* (as configured in
    *slurm.conf*). The argument to the program will be the names of
    nodes to be

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

## Problems[¶](#problems "Permalink to this headline")

Please check first of all whether there is an answer to your question in the FAQ. If not, please request support with your details and

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Queue Wait Time
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports that a short GPU job has been waiting much longer than usual. This does not by itself indicate an account or script fault; queue time can increase when demand is high, and queued jobs gain priority over time.

Cancelling and resubmitting is counterproductive because it removes the job's accumulated queue priority.

ACTION

1) Reassure the user that long waits can happen when demand is high, even for jobs that usually start quickly.
2) Advise the user not to cancel and resubmit queued jobs unless necessary.
3) If they want a rough estimate, suggest:
   `squeue -u <CRSid> --start`
4) Clarify that starting an interactive job does not change the priority of an already queued batch job.
5) Escalate only if there is evidence of scheduler malfunction rather than normal contention.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Kai,

From the information provided, there does not appear to be anything obviously wrong with your job submission. Queue times can change significantly depending on current demand, so a job that usually starts quickly may occasionally wait much longer.

Please avoid cancelling and resubmitting the job unless necessary, as that resets its place in the queue and removes any priority it has accumulated while waiting.

If you want a rough estimate of when a queued job may start, you can run:

  squeue -u <your CRSid> --start

Also, starting an interactive job will not alter the priority of an existing queued batch job.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not promise a fixed start time.
- Do not imply scheduler fault without evidence.
- Warn users that cancelling/resubmitting resets queue age.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Scheduling / Queue Time
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an unusually long queue time for a GPU job [8]. The job was submitted with a request for 1 node and 1 GPU, with a time limit of 20 minutes [User Query]. Similar issues have been reported in the past, where non-paying jobs were delayed due to higher priority paying jobs [2][8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Please inform the user that the delay is likely due to the high priority of paying jobs and the limited availability of GPU resources [2][8]. Advise the user to be patient and not to cancel and resubmit the job, as this would be counterproductive [2][8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Are there any specific requirements or deadlines for the job that need to be met?
- Has the user considered submitting the job to a different partition or using a different resource allocation?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about the delay in your GPU job. We understand that this can be frustrating, especially when working on a project with a tight deadline. Unfortunately, the delay is likely due to the high priority of paying jobs and the limited availability of GPU resources. We advise you to be patient and not to cancel and resubmit the job, as this would be counterproductive. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Non-paying jobs may experience delays due to higher priority paying jobs [2][8].
- Cancelling and resubmitting a job can be counterproductive and may lead to further delays [2][8].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : HPCSSUP-59843
[8] : HPCSSUP-59877

# [Slurm Workload Manager](/)

[SchedMD](https://www.schedmd.com/)

# Job Reason Codes

These reason codes can be used to identify why a pending job has not yet been
started by the scheduler. There may be multiple reasons why a job cannot start,
in which case only the reason that was encountered by the attempted scheduling
method will be displayed. Refer to the [Scheduling Configuration Guide](sched_config.html) for more details.

## Common Reasons

* **AssocGrp\*** — The job's association has reached an aggregate
  [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **AssocMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a maximum
  [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for the
  requested association.
* **BeginTime** — The job's earliest [start time](sbatch.html#OPT_begin) has not yet been reached.
* **Dependency** — This job has a
  [dependency](sbatch.html#OPT_dependency) on another job that has not
  been satisfied.
* **Max\*PerAccount** — A portion of the job request exceeds the
  per-Account [limit](resource_limits.html) on the job's
  [QOS](qos.html).
* **Priority** — One of more higher
  [priority](priority_multifactor.html) jobs exist for the partition
  associated with the job or for the [advanced
  reservation](reservations.html).
* **QOSGrp\*** — The job's [QOS](qos.html) has reached an
  aggregate [limit](resource_limits.html).
* **QOSMax\*** — A portion of the job request exceeds a
  maximum [limit](resource_limits.html) (e.g., PerJob, PerNode) for
  the requested [QOS](qos.html).
* **Resources** — The resources requested by the job are not
  available (e.g., already used by other jobs).

## All Reasons

* **AccountingPolicy** — Fallback reason when others not matched.
* **AccountNotAllowed** — Job is in an account not allowed in a
  partition.
* **AssocGrpBB** — The job's association has reached its aggregate
  Burst Buffer limit.
* **AssocGrpBBMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by past,
  present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBBRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached the
  maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for Burst Buffers by
  currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpBilling** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Billing limit.
* **AssocGrpBillingMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of
  a resource by past, present and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpBillingRunMinutes** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for the Billing value of a
  resource by currently running jobs.
* **AssocGrpCpuLimit** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate CPU limit.
* **AssocGrpCPUMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by past, present
  and future jobs.
* **AssocGrpCPURunMinutesLimit** — The job's association has reached
  the maximum number of minutes allowed in aggregate for CPUs by currently
  running jobs.
* **AssocGrpEnergy** — The job's association has reached its
  aggregate Energy limit.
* **AssocGrpEnergyMinutes** — The

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

```
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
```

where 1 <= N <= 4.

Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA Ampere A100 GPUs, with 1000GB host RAM and two AMD EPYC 64-core processors.

Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 3 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.

### Submitting the job to the queuing system[¶](#submitting-the-job-to-the-queuing-system "Permalink to this headline")

The command sbatch is used to submit jobs, e.g.

```
sbatch submission_script
```

The command will return a unique job identifier, which is used to query and control the job and to identify output. See the man page (*man sbatch*) for more options.

The following more complex example submits a job array with index values between 1 and 7 with a step size of 2 (i.e. 1, 3, 5 and 7) to the project STARS-SL2-CPU:

```
sbatch --array=1-7:2 -A STARS-SL2-CPU submission_script
```

### Deleting jobs[¶](#deleting-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

To cancel a job (either running or still queuing) use scancel:

```
scancel <jobid>
```

The <jobid> is printed when the job is submitted, alternatively use the commands squeue, qstat or showq to obtain the job ID.

## Array jobs[¶](#array-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Array jobs allow the submission of multiple similar jobs. Array jobs should be prefered to multiple `sbatch` calls in a loop. An example of a

[TICKET_SUMMARY] gpu long queue times

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support group,
I hope this email finds you well.
I am a relatively new user of the hpc cluster and have been lately facing long queue times for the jobs submitted with the ampere gpu. For example, a job with the following specifications will still be on the queue even after a day of being submitted:
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=32
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=03:30:00
I was wondering if this is something to be expected or if it’s to do with anything from my side?
Thank you for your time!
Best wishes,
Sofia

QOSL is implemented by two SLURM QoS definitions: icelake-long and cclake-long have `cpul`, which is restricted to 512 cpus per user; ampere-long has `gpul`, which is restricted to 32 GPUs per user.

In order to apply for access to QOSL, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk detailing why this mode of usage is necessary, and explaining why checkpointing is not a practical option for your application.

## Submitting long jobs[¶](#submitting-long-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Use of QOSL is tied to the -long partitions, therefore once given access it is necessary only to specify this partition - e.g.

```
sbatch -t 7-0:0:0 -p icelake-long -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
```

where 1 <= N <= 4.

Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA Ampere A100 GPUs, with 1000GB host RAM and two AMD EPYC 64-core processors.

Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from requesting more than 3 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it rejects a job outright.

### Submitting the job to the queuing system[¶](#submitting-the-job-to-the-queuing-system "Permalink to this headline")

The command sbatch is used to submit jobs, e.g.

```
sbatch submission_script
```

The command will return a unique job identifier, which is used to query and control the job and to identify output. See the man page (*man sbatch*) for more options.

The following more complex example submits a job array with index values between 1 and 7 with a step size of 2 (i.e. 1, 3, 5 and 7) to the project STARS-SL2-CPU:

```
sbatch --array=1-7:2 -A STARS-SL2-CPU submission_script
```

### Deleting jobs[¶](#deleting-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

To cancel a job (either running or still queuing) use scancel:

```
scancel <jobid>
```

The <jobid> is printed when the job is submitted, alternatively use the commands squeue, qstat or showq to obtain the job ID.

## Array jobs[¶](#array-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

Array jobs allow the submission of multiple similar jobs. Array jobs should be prefered to multiple `sbatch` calls in a loop. An example of a

<BEGIN_TICKET>
[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-59877
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2023-08-09T05:16:58.884+0100
[SUMMARY] gpu long queue times

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC support group,
I hope this email finds you well.
I am a relatively new user of the hpc cluster and have been lately facing long queue times for the jobs submitted with the ampere gpu. For example, a job with the following specifications will still be on the queue even after a day of being submitted:
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=32
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=03:30:00
I was wondering if this is something to be expected or if it’s to do with anything from my side?
Thank you for your time!
Best wishes,
Sofia
–
Sofia Kapsiani
PhD Student (Molecular Neuroscience Group)
Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
University of Cambridge
Email: sk2067@cam.ac.uk<[sk2067@cam.ac.uk](mailto:sk2067@cam.ac.uk)>

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Sofia,
There is nothing wrong with your account or jobs, except that they are at the non-paying service level and there is a lot of paying activity which has higher priority. 
Please bear in mind that each paying job can occupy its resources for 36 hours. Please be patient and don’t be tempted to cancel and resubmit - since all jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, that would be counterproductive. 
Your jobs will run when resources become available.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Stuart,
Thank you very much for confirming!
Best wishes,
Sofia
</MESSAGE>

<END_TICKET>

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Authentication / SSH Login / Password Clarification
Technical Action Required: No (user guidance only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User is attempting to SSH to login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk and reports not knowing their HPC password, stating they may never have set one.

On CSD3, there is no separate HPC-specific password for internal users. Authentication uses the user’s UIS/Raven password, followed by the appropriate CSD3 TOTP token for SSH access.

ACTION

1) Clarify authentication model
   - HPC password = UIS/Raven password.
   - There is no separate password to set during activation.

2) Clarify SSH login sequence
   When connecting via:
     ssh <CRSid>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk

   The user should:
     a) Enter their current UIS/Raven password.
     b) Enter their TOTP code associated with CSD3 SSH (typically labelled “CSD3:ssh” or similar in their authenticator app).

3) If login still fails
   Ask:
     - Have you recently changed your UIS/Raven password?
     - Do you have the correct CSD3 SSH MFA token configured?
     - What exact error message appears after entering password/TOTP?

4) Do NOT:
   - Reset password directly.
   - Provide or request passwords.
   - Create a separate HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC password

Dear Fay,

There is no separate HPC password to set up. Your HPC login password is the same as your UIS/Raven password.

When connecting via SSH, please enter your current UIS/Raven password when prompted, followed by the TOTP code associated with your CSD3 SSH login (usually labelled “CSD3:ssh” in your authenticator app).

If you continue to experience issues, please let us know the exact error message shown after entering your credentials.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Never request or store user passwords.
- Do not imply a separate HPC password exists.
- Escalate only if authentication failures persist after confirming correct credential usage.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is having trouble logging in to their HPC account and does not remember their password [1]. The user has tried using their Raven password, but it is not working [8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the user should try resetting their password using the UIS password management application [6]. If the user is still having trouble, they can try using their CRSid and Raven password to log in [7]. If the issue persists, the user may need to contact the HPC support team to have their account reset [8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried resetting your password using the UIS password management application [6]?
- Are you using the correct CRSid and Raven password to log in [7]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your help. I have tried resetting my password using the UIS password management application, but I am still having trouble logging in. I will try using my CRSid and Raven password to log in.
Best regards,
Fay

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users should always use their UIS password to log in to their HPC account [7].
- Users should never share their password with anyone [1].

[6] : HPCSSUP-68215
[7] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html::chunk::0001
[8] : HPCSSUP-43190

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a

## Problem: Password Required

**Problem**

Password required.

**Cause**

The Intel® MPI Library uses SSH mechanism to access remote nodes. SSH requires password and this may cause the MPI application hang.

**Solution**

1. Check the SSH settings.
2. Make sure that the passwordless authorization by public keys is enabled and configured.

# PI Dawn Access[¶](#pi-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## PI Accessing Portal[¶](#pi-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (IdP of last resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to ensure you are

[TICKET_SUMMARY] HPC password

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC team,
I am trying to access my hpc account, which I usually accessed by `ssh hpc`, having everything configure in the .ssh/config file.
I am now being asked a Password, which unfortunately I don't remember.
Is there an easy solution to this.
Thank you and best wishes,

passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the

* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a

## Problem: Password Required

**Problem**

Password required.

**Cause**

The Intel® MPI Library uses SSH mechanism to access remote nodes. SSH requires password and this may cause the MPI application hang.

**Solution**

1. Check the SSH settings.
2. Make sure that the passwordless authorization by public keys is enabled and configured.

passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

benchmark_annotation.summary: SRCP Epi platform - storage query

condition.preset_description: Frozen validity-aware reranker over local docs, external docs, and tickets.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Storage Architecture / RFS
Technical Action Required: No (clarification only)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

FOCUS OF RESPONSE
User’s second query:

“My understanding is that somehow storage for SRCP is provisioned on RFS. Is this RFS dedicated hardware that is isolated for use by a particular platform? Or does it work differently? In which case, is there the option to have dedicated RCS hardware, which will be cheaper?”

QUICK ASSESSMENT
SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS-backed storage that is logically and operationally isolated per platform/project. It is not pooled with general-purpose RDS/RCS environments, and users cannot directly mount external RCS storage within SRCP. There is no model for attaching separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a cheaper alternative.

ACTION

1) Clarify storage model
   - SRCP storage is provisioned as isolated RFS shares associated specifically with SRCP projects.
   - These shares are separated from other storage systems and not user-configurable.
   - The isolation is by design due to SRCP security requirements.

2) Clarify hardware/cost question
   - SRCP storage is not dynamically attached from arbitrary RCS pools.
   - There is no option to provision separate “dedicated RCS hardware” for SRCP at a reduced cost.
   - SRCP environments have a defined architecture and approved storage pathways only.

3) If cost optimisation is the underlying concern
   - Suggest reviewing retention strategy (e.g., archive/export workflow outside SRCP if appropriate).
   - Explain that external storage (e.g., RCS) would require controlled export and re-import, not live mounting.

4) Do NOT:
   - Suggest bypassing SRCP isolation.
   - Imply that SRCP storage can be merged with other RCS/RDS resources.
   - Promise alternative hardware provisioning unless formally approved.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: SRCP storage architecture

Dear Tom,

SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS shares that are logically and operationally isolated for SRCP projects. These are separate from other RCS/RDS storage systems and are not directly accessible or mountable from outside the platform.

It is not possible to attach separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a lower-cost alternative. The SRCP architecture and its storage pathways are fixed by design due to the security requirements of the platform.

If you are looking to reduce storage costs, one option would be to export data from SRCP and archive it to a nominated RCS project outside the platform, but this would not be a live integration.

Please let us know if you would like to discuss possible workflows for archiving data.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Maintain SRCP security model integrity.
- Do not suggest storage sharing across security domains.
- Avoid speculative statements about hardware provisioning beyond documented architecture.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Archive Management [2]
Technical Action Required: Yes [2]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [2]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
RCS (Research Cold Store) is available as an archive storage option on CSD3, providing a location for inactive, archive data for long-term storage [2]. RFS (Research File Share) is also available for highly resilient group-storage with hourly backups [2]. 

ACTION
To archive old projects, consider purchasing an RCS store or an RFS project through the self-service portal [2]. For large quantities of data, contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss setup and migration to CSD3 [8]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the estimated size of the data to be archived?
- Are there any specific requirements for data resilience or backup?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To archive your old projects, you can purchase an RCS store or an RFS project through the self-service portal: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk [2]. If you have large quantities of data, please contact us at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss setup and migration to CSD3 [8]. 
Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that you have the necessary permissions and approvals before archiving or migrating data [2].
- Be aware of the costs associated with purchasing storage projects on CSD3 [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html::chunk::0004
[8] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/wbic.html::chunk::0006
metrics.user_input:
Hi,
We would like to archive some old projects on our platform. Is RFS the only storage option available, or is RCS available from within the SRCP?
Thanks
Tom

Subject: Re: SRCP storage architecture

Dear Tom,

SRCP storage is provisioned using RFS shares that are logically and operationally isolated for SRCP projects. These are separate from other RCS/RDS storage systems and are not directly accessible or mountable from outside the platform.

It is not possible to attach separate “dedicated RCS hardware” to SRCP as a lower-cost alternative. The SRCP architecture and its storage pathways are fixed by design due to the security requirements of the platform.

If you are looking to reduce storage costs, one option would be to export data from SRCP and archive it to a nominated RCS project outside the platform, but this would not be a live integration.

Please let us know if you would like to discuss possible workflows for archiving data.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Maintain SRCP security model integrity.
- Do not suggest storage sharing across security domains.
- Avoid speculative statements about hardware provisioning beyond documented architecture.

QUICK ASSESSMENT
RCS (Research Cold Store) is available as an archive storage option on CSD3, providing a location for inactive, archive data for long-term storage [2]. RFS (Research File Share) is also available for highly resilient group-storage with hourly backups [2]. 

ACTION
To archive old projects, consider purchasing an RCS store or an RFS project through the self-service portal [2]. For large quantities of data, contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss setup and migration to CSD3 [8]. 

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the estimated size of the data to be archived?
- Are there any specific requirements for data resilience or backup?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To archive your old projects, you can purchase an RCS store or an RFS project through the self-service portal: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk [2]. If you have large quantities of data, please contact us at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss setup and migration to CSD3 [8]. 
Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

- Be aware of the costs associated with purchasing storage projects on CSD3 [2].

Warning

The WBIC platform contained a number of different storage areas for
users, all of which will be **retired** with the closure of the platform.

All data should be migrated to an alternative storage platform on
CSD3 or downloaded to another location before the platform is
shut-down.

Below we outline alternative services on CSD3 for each storage area that
are available for purchase so that you can replicate the WBIC
environment as best as possible.

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.

This data will require being moved to an RDS (scratch), RCS
(archive) or RFS (resilient, backup) project on CSD3.

Group data should be purchased as a group, which would typically be
done by the PI for your research group. Additionally, given the
quantities of data involved, we can perform the data migration for
your group so that it is carried out as efficiently as possible.

Important

Please encourage your PI to get in touch with us at
support@hpc.cam.ac.uk to discuss how we can assist the setup and
migration of group data to CSD3.

We will be attempting to contact all known PIs to assist in this
process, but being proactive here can help ensure that we can make
this transition as seamless as possible for your group.

#### 3. Purchase any required storage projects on CSD3[¶](#purchase-any-required-storage-projects-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

Purchases can be made via our [storage portal](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk).
Projects greater than 100TB in size are eligible for a volume
pricing discount.

#### 4. Migrate individual files to CSD3[¶](#migrate-individual-files-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

For relatively modest quantities of data less than 1TB, you should be
able to migrate these yourself with `rsync`. An example command to
use would be,

```
user@wbic-gate-3:~$ rsync --verbose --info=progress2 --archive --no-o --no-g \
 --whole-file --update --inplace --rsh \
 "ssh -T -x -o Compression=no -c aes128-gcm@openssh.com" \
 ~/

#### /lustre[¶](#lustre "Permalink to this headline")

The WBIC dedicated Lustre filesystem (/lustre) is replaced by our [RDS
storage service](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store)
which provides equivalent large-scale scratch storage.

All CSD3 users get a free 1TB personal scratch space, but all
group-shared capacity must be purchased through our [Storage self-service
gateway](https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/storage/RDS/)
Owners of group directories will be contacted separately to make arrangements.

#### /home[¶](#home "Permalink to this headline")

All WBIC users had 100GB of /home space, however on CSD3 this is reduced
to 40GB. Users with usage greater than 40GB will be required to either
reduce their usage under this amount, or to purchase additional RFS or
RDS storage and move excess data to these areas.

#### /data[¶](#data "Permalink to this headline")

WBIC users also had access to the /data storage location, which benefited
from the same resilient storage platform as /home, including hourly
snapshots and data replication, designed for datasets requiring durable
backups.

A similar

[SUMMARY] rfs-fjg28-cc-media

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC team,  
  
Thank you for all your help with our RFS storage.  
We understand this storage will expire on the 17 May 2022. 
  
Please can we ask for your help to carry out the following plan to shrink the RFS storage size by transferring the majority of our data to an RCS store?  
  
1. Such that please can we set up a new RCS store
2. Please can your team then help to transfer the following files from our RFS store to the new RCS store?
 
  
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/2017-CC06 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/CUH-2018-CC06 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/CUH-IC-CC05 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/CUH-SD-CC06 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/CUH-Study1.0-normals-CC05 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/ CUH-Study1.0-normals-CC05-Detector-ID 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/IC-CC05 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-2015-CC08 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-2016-CC08 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-2017-CC08 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-IC-CC07 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-SD-CC08 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/NNUH-Study1.1-normals-CC07 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/OPTIMAM-CC06 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/OPTIMAM-CC06-Detector-ID 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/SD-CC06 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/Study1.0_images 
rfs-fjg28-cc-media/CC-MEDIA/Study2.0_images 
  
1. And then reduce the size of the RFS to account for the size needed for just those remaining files plus 1TB?
 
  
Can I please check that that would reduce the size of the RFS before the renewal deadline thus reducing the cost for next year?  
  
Please can we also enquire regarding the guarantee of the lifecycle of the RCS store?  
  
With best wishes and many thanks,  
  
Sarah

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Sarah,
This is definitely something we can assist with.
We can do a capacity analysis of the directories that you have listed so that we can quote you for a new RCS space. Once that is paid for and created we can facilitate the movement of the data on our end.
Once the copy is complete we can delete the copied data from RFS to allow for the shrinkage. At this point we'll know the remaining usage and we'll round it up to the nearest TB and +1TB as you request. And this is the number we'll shrink the RFS license to in the portal, so then you can renew for the correct cost using the portal.
If things are getting a little tight for time, we can invoke the shrink of RFS in the portal earlier, and delay reflecting the changes on the file-system until all the data is in the correct locations. The portal is mainly for book keeping and doesn't directly interact with file system, so we can intervene in cases like this.
Regarding your RCS lifecycle question can you clarify what you mean by lifecycle? Are you referring to our plans for the lifetime of RCS as a service?
Best,
Dominic
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Dominic, 
Thank you for getting back to us regarding this query. 
That would be brilliant if you can please provide us with a quote regarding the size needed for the RCS store. 
It is also good news that your team can facilitate the transfer of the data from the RFS to the RCS and shrink the RFS size to provide us with an updated quote for the year renewal of the RFS store. 
Yes, our question pertaining to the RCS was about the "plans for the lifetime of RCS as a service", please can you provide us with any additional information?
With best wishes and many thanks, 
Sarah

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS & RFS / Provisioning
Technical Action Required: No (user action pending)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (access pending)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
A new user (vj292) has been added to both an RDS and RFS-NFS project via the self-service portal but remains in “Provisioning” state. The most common cause of this status is that the user has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions in the UIS Self Service portal. Until this step is completed, the provisioning workflow cannot proceed and access will not be activated.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm provisioning state (internal)
   - Verify that the user appears as “Provisioning” on the relevant RDS and RFS projects.
   - Check whether Terms & Conditions acceptance is outstanding.

2) Instruct user to accept Terms & Conditions
   Ask the new member (vj292) to:
   - Visit: https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/
   - Click on each relevant storage project:
       • rds-djh1002-hodson-rds
       • rfs-djh1002-hodson-rfs-nfs
   - Accept the storage Terms & Conditions for each project.

3) Explain workflow
   - Once Terms & Conditions are accepted, a provisioning request is automatically generated.
   - HPC Support will then activate access.
   - All relevant parties will receive automatic email confirmation once provisioning completes.

4) Do NOT:
   - Manually override provisioning before Terms & Conditions are accepted.
   - Escalate unless provisioning remains stalled after acceptance.

IF PROVISIONING DOES NOT COMPLETE AFTER ACCEPTANCE

- Confirm acceptance timestamp in portal.
- Check whether an automatic provisioning ticket was generated.
- Escalate to storage administration only if backend provisioning fails after acceptance.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: Provisioning status for new lab member

Dear Joanna,

Thank you for your message.

Victor’s access is currently showing as “Provisioning” because he has not yet accepted the storage service Terms & Conditions.

Could he please visit:

  https://selfservice.uis.cam.ac.uk/account/

and click on the relevant project links to accept the Terms & Conditions for each storage project?

Once this is completed, a provisioning request will automatically be generated and we will activate his access. All parties will receive confirmation by email once this has been processed.

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not bypass the mandatory Terms & Conditions acceptance process.
- Do not grant access before provisioning workflow completes.
- Ensure both RDS and RFS projects are covered if both were requested.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / User Provisioning [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [4]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [2]
Urgency: Medium [4]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has added Victor to their lab RDS and RFS-NFS space, but Victor's status is still showing as "Provisioning" in the self-service portal [1]. This issue is similar to a previously resolved ticket where the user needed to accept the terms and conditions of using RDS storage [4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check if Victor has received an email with a link to accept the terms and conditions of using RDS storage [4].
2. If Victor has not received the email, try deleting and re-adding him to the project in the self-service portal [1].
3. If the issue persists, check the group ownership and permissions of the project directory [4].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Has Victor accepted the terms and conditions of using RDS storage?
- Is Victor part of the correct group (rds-djh1002-hodson-rds-users) [4]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Please check if you have received an email with a link to accept the terms and conditions of using RDS storage. If not, try deleting and re-adding yourself to the project in the self-service portal. If the issue persists, please let us know and we will investigate further.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Users must accept the terms and conditions of using RDS storage before they can access the project [4].
- Group ownership and permissions must be correctly set for the project directory [4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-36160
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-pi.html::chunk::0004
[4] : HPCSSUP-88461

Many thanks for your help.
Best wishes, 
Joanna

MFA please see our user documentation page:https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html)

You will now have access to the portal.

**Accepting the Invitation**

Upon entering the system, a pop-up notification will prompt you to accept the invitation. To continue, simply click on “Accept Invitation.”

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation.png)

**Once accepted, users need to wait approximately 5 seconds for synchronization. A pop-up notification will appear at the top right of the screen confirming that the invitation has been accepted.**

Upon acceptance, you will be directed to the home page. The next step involves reading and agreeing to the terms and conditions.

Dawn has several policies that must be accepted before you can utilize any of its services. Once you log in, a list of these policies will be presented for your acceptance. You will encounter them on screens that resemble the following format.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/accept_terms.png)](../_images/accept_terms.png)

Upon accepting the policy, you will be online and ready to begin using the system.

## PI Managing Projects[¶](#pi-managing-projects "Permalink to this headline")

**Understanding roles and permissions for Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal:**

There are three roles you can have on a project:

* Principal Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team

Dawn has several policies that must be accepted before you can utilize any of its services. Once you log in, a list of these policies will be presented for your acceptance. You will encounter them on screens that resemble the following format.

Upon accepting the policy, you will be online and ready to begin using the system.

  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team

benchmark_annotation.summary: Getting write permission to rds project

condition.preset_description: Frozen validity-aware reranker over local docs, external docs, and tickets.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RDS Permissions / Project Roles
Technical Action Required: No (permission governance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium (write access blocked)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User requests read/write access to an RDS project and assumes only one Data Manager can exist. On RDS projects, multiple Data Managers are allowed. If the user is currently a Data User, write capability should be granted by project governance (Data Owner/Data Manager) either by role reassignment or by applying permissions according to RDS permissions guidance.

ACTION

1) Clarify role model
   - Data Owners can appoint multiple Data Managers.
   - Data Users cannot self-assign elevated permissions.

2) Direct user to project governance path
   - Ask user to contact the Data Owner or existing Data Manager for:
     a) role change to Data Manager, or
     b) permission grant aligned with RDS ACL guidance.

3) Provide documentation link
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Emilia,

Data Owners can add as many Data Managers as required on an RDS project.

If you are currently a Data User, you will need to ask the Data Owner or a Data Manager to either:
1) reassign your role to Data Manager, or  
2) grant the required permissions in line with the RDS permissions guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rds/permissions.html

Best wishes,  
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not change project roles/permissions without Data Owner or Data Manager authorisation.
- Do not imply Helpdesk can bypass project governance for access control.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [1]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Portal Action Required: Yes [2]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting read and write access to the RDS project rds-bru20-ukbb with project ID isCN5CtgAac [1]. By default, users only have read access to RDS folders, and data managers need to create a subdirectory for users to write to [7].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To resolve this issue, the data managers of the project need to create a subdirectory within the RDS folder and grant the user write access to that subdirectory [7]. Alternatively, the user can be added as a data manager to the project, but this requires careful consideration of the project's permissions and access control [1][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm the current data managers of the project rds-bru20-ukbb [1]?
- Have you tried creating a subdirectory within the RDS folder and granting the user write access to that subdirectory [7]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To resolve the issue with read and write access to the RDS project rds-bru20-ukbb, we recommend that the data managers of the project create a subdirectory within the RDS folder and grant you write access to that subdirectory [7]. Alternatively, we can discuss adding you as a data manager to the project, but this requires careful consideration of the project's permissions and access control [1][5].
Please let us know how you would like to proceed.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- By default, users only have read access to RDS folders, and data managers need to create a subdirectory for users to write to [7].
- Careful consideration of the project's permissions and access control is required when adding users as data managers [1][5].

benchmark_annotation.summary: HPC Job Scheduling Error

condition.preset_description: Frozen validity-aware reranker over local docs, external docs, and tickets.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Scheduling / Service Level Priority
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low–Medium (long wait time but system functioning)

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports a GPU job taking almost two weeks to start and asks whether there is an issue with their setup.

From the job script:
- Account: SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
- Partition: ampere
- GPUs requested: 4
- Wall time: 12:00:00

There are no configuration errors visible in the submission script. The most likely cause of delay is scheduler priority and service level.

Key scheduling principles:
- SL3 accounts operate at the non-paying service level.
- Paying service-level jobs receive higher priority.
- Priority increases over time while waiting (aging factor).
- Large GPU requests (e.g. 4 GPUs) may experience longer queue times depending on demand.
- Cancelling and resubmitting resets priority and can increase overall wait time.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)

1) Confirm no configuration errors
   - The script contains valid SBATCH directives.
   - Correct partition and account syntax used.
   - No obvious resource mis-specification.

2) Explain scheduling behaviour
   - Jobs are prioritised according to service level and fair-share policy.
   - Paying activity can temporarily delay SL3 jobs.
   - Jobs steadily gain priority the longer they wait.

3) Provide best practice guidance
   - Do not cancel and resubmit while waiting.
   - Consider reducing resource request (e.g., fewer GPUs) if flexible.
   - Use `squeue -u <CRSid>` to monitor queue position.
   - Use `scontrol show job <jobid>` to inspect priority factors if desired.

4) Do NOT:
   - Escalate unless there is evidence of scheduler malfunction.
   - Promise faster scheduling.
   - Modify account priority.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the Job ID?
- Has the job been pending (PD) the entire time?
- What does `scontrol show job <jobid>` report under Reason= ?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Subject: Re: HPC Job Scheduling Delay

Dear Amogh,

From the information provided, there is no obvious sign of a problem with your account or submission script. Your job is running under an SL3 (non-paying) GPU account, and at times there can be significant demand from higher-priority paying accounts.

Please note that each paying job can occupy its allocated GPUs for up to 36 hours. Jobs steadily increase in priority the longer they wait, so cancelling and resubmitting would be counterproductive as it resets that priority.

Your job will run when sufficient resources become available.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not imply scheduler malfunction without evidence.
- Do not recommend priority manipulation.
- Avoid suggesting users circumvent service-level policy.
- Reinforce correct queueing behaviour and expectations.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: HPC / Job Submission
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has submitted a job on the HPC and it took almost 2 weeks to get scheduled [1]. The job script is provided, and it requests 4 GPUs per node on the ampere partition [1]. The retrieved documents suggest that the ampere partition has a limited number of nodes and a high priority for paying jobs [2][4]. The user's job is likely being delayed due to the high priority of other jobs and the limited availability of nodes [2][4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the job script and submission parameters to ensure they are correct and optimized for the ampere partition [1][6].
2. Check the job's status and priority in the queue using squeue or qstat [8].
3. Consider resubmitting the job with a higher priority or adjusting the submission parameters to improve scheduling [2][4].
4. If the issue persists, investigate potential node or resource allocation issues [5][7].

QUESTIONS TO ASSESS THE SITUATION FURTHER
- Can you provide more information about the job's priority and submission parameters?
- Have you tried resubmitting the job or adjusting the submission parameters?
- Are there any specific error messages or output files that may indicate the cause of the delay?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reaching out to us about your job submission issue. We apologize for the delay and are happy to help you troubleshoot. Please try resubmitting the job with the suggested adjustments and let us know if the issue persists. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to contact us.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Be cautious when adjusting job submission parameters to avoid over-allocating resources or causing node conflicts [1][6].
- Ensure that all job submissions comply with the HPC usage policies and guidelines [2][4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/a100.html::chunk::0001
[2] : HPCSSUP-70211
[4] : HPCSSUP-59877
[5] : https://slurm.schedmd.com/prolog_epilog.html::chunk::0001
[6] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/batch.html::chunk::0005
[7] : HPCSSUP-87714
[8] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html::chunk::0008
metrics.user_input:
To whom it may concern,
I have submitted a job on the HPC, and it took almost 2 weeks to get scheduled. I was wondering if there were any issues with my setup. The job script I used is below.
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH -J job_gpu_1
#SBATCH -o /home/av670/logfiles/job_gpu_1.log
#SBATCH -A SVOBODA-SL3-GPU
