metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login Node Health / Temporary Filesystem
Technical Action Required: Yes (diagnose node-specific issue)
Escalation Required: Optional (if persistent)
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
User reports compiler warning that `/tmp` is full and asks whether this relates to their own quota. `/tmp` is node-local temporary storage and can fill independently of the user’s `/home` or project quota.

ACTION
1) Explain what `/tmp` is:
   - temporary local filesystem on the node,
   - not the same as user/project quota shown by `quota`.
2) Ask user to report the node where error occurs (`hostname`).
3) Suggest immediate workaround: try compiling on another login node.
4) Escalate if issue persists across multiple nodes or for multiple users.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Loren,

The warning about `/tmp` means temporary space on the current node is full. This is separate from your `/home`/project quota, so your `quota` output can still look normal.

Please share the output of:
`hostname`

As a workaround, please try compiling on a different login node. If the issue appears on multiple nodes, let us know and we will escalate.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not assume user quota issue when warning explicitly references `/tmp`.
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Compilation / Temporary Space
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The error message "icc: warning #10352: The directory '/tmp' is full.  Please check disk space." indicates that the temporary directory /tmp is full, which is preventing the compilation of the code [3][5]. This issue has been encountered before, and the solution was to clear some space in the /tmp partition [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
Check the current usage of the /tmp partition and clear any unnecessary files to free up space. If the issue persists, investigate which user or process is causing the /tmp partition to fill up and take appropriate action [3][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Which node are you currently working on?
- Can you provide more details about your compilation process and the code you are trying to compile?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
The /tmp directory is currently full, which is preventing your code from compiling. We will clear some space in the /tmp partition to resolve this issue. If you continue to experience problems, please let us know and we will investigate further.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- The /tmp directory is a temporary storage space and should not be used for long-term storage [4].
- Users should ensure that they are not storing unnecessary files in the /tmp directory [4].

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

Please see the example job submission scripts under `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM`. There are example scripts for launching an MPI application on CPU or GPU via

`CUDA_TILE_TEMP_DIR` to configure the directory
for storing temporary files.

Set `CUDA_TILE_CACHE_DIR` to configure the directory
for the bytecode-to-cubin disk cache. Compiled cubins
are cached here to avoid recompilation of unchanged
kernels. Set to `0`, `off`, `none`, or an empty
string to disable caching. Defaults to
`~/.cache/cutile-python`.

Set `CUDA_TILE_CACHE_SIZE` to configure the maximum
disk cache size in bytes. Oldest entries are evicted
when the cache exceeds this limit. Defaults to
2 GB (2147483648).

latter are performant and scalable, and *it is on these that active data to be read or written by jobs should be placed* (not under /home which is more suited to code, post processed results, reports etc).

The hpc-work directories provide free, 1TB storage for the personal use of Cambridge researchers. This storage cannot be shared with other users - please purchase shared storage from the [Research Data Store (RDS)](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store). The hpc-work directories themselves are also provided by the RDS service. National Facility (DiRAC/Tier2/IRIS) users may already have additional project areas visible under /rds/user/<username> and ~/rds provided from pre-paid RDS storage which are shared between members of the same project.

Please note that for general users we currently provide two tiers only of non-transient storage (i.e. storage not limited to the runtime of a single job), namely */home* and *hpc-work* (the latter possibly supplemented by additional RDS storage areas if purchased). As stated above RDS directories are based on Lustre and are the correct targets for job I/O. The term *scratch* space if encountered should be understood to refer to these Lustre storage areas.

## Disk quotas[¶](#disk-quotas "Permalink to this headline")

Each user has a limit on the amount of file storage he/she can use in the home directory, via quotas. Quotas on /home are currently set at 50 GB per user. Use the command quota to list your current usage - this reports for /home, /rds/user/<username>/hpc-work and any RDS projects you may have. A \* symbol next

## Error Message: No such file or Directory

**Error Message**

```
[proxy:0:0@node1] HYD_spawn
(../../../../../src/pm/i_hydra/libhydra/spawn/hydra_spawn.c:113): execvp error on file {path to binary file}/{binary file} (No such file or directory)
```

**Cause**

Wrong path to the binary file or the binary file does not exist on the `node01`. The name of the binary file is misprinted or the shared space cannot be

to this value indicates that you have met or exceeded your quota (listed in GB under quota), and will need to reduce usage as soon as possible to below the quota value.

There are 1TB quotas in force on /rds/usr/<username>/hpc-work. For some filesystems, the limit value is slightly greater than the quota value (the former is also known as the hard limit, whereas the latter is the soft limit). It is possible (but not desirable) to exceed the soft limit, but not the hard limit. Exceeding the soft limit is allowed for 7 days, but if the over-quota condition is not corrected after 7 days, creation of new files and directories on the filesystem will become impossible. This happens immediately the soft limit is met if the hard and soft limits are equal. It is essential to resolve this situation as quickly as possible to prevent job errors and (in the case of /home) more subtle malfunctions of the account.

Additionally /rds/usr/<username>/hpc-work has a quota limit on the number of files that can be created, which is limited to 1 million files.

More RDS storage can be purchased - full details of this service are accessible [here](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store).

## Compiling[¶](#compiling "Permalink to this headline")

Note that the `default-X` module, which is loaded by default on nodes of type X, arranges for `mpicc`, `mpif90` etc to be found and to use the recommended compilers and MPI implementation automatically when invoked. These wrapper commands supply the correct flags for compiling with the particular MPI implementation to which they belong.

When compiling code, it is usually possible to remove any direct MPI library references in your Makefile as mpicc & mpif90 will take care of these details. In the Makefile, simply set

```
CC=mpicc
```

etc, or define

```
export CC=mpicc
```

etc in the shell before compilation.

If some required libraries are missing, please let us know and we can try to install them centrally (as a module).

## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

Please see the example job submission scripts under `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM`. There are example scripts for launching an MPI application on CPU or GPU via

`CUDA_TILE_TEMP_DIR` to configure the directory
for storing temporary files.

Set `CUDA_TILE_CACHE_DIR` to configure the directory
for the bytecode-to-cubin disk cache. Compiled cubins
are cached here to avoid recompilation of unchanged
kernels. Set to `0`, `off`, `none`, or an empty
string to disable caching. Defaults to
`~/.cache/cutile-python`.

Set `CUDA_TILE_CACHE_SIZE` to configure the maximum
disk cache size in bytes. Oldest entries are evicted
when the cache exceeds this limit. Defaults to
2 GB (2147483648).

Hi Conor,
It seems the /tmp partition on login-e-14 where you are working had been filled up by another user. I've cleared some s
...[truncated for evaluator]

latter are performant and scalable, and *it is on these that active data to be read or written by jobs should be placed* (not under /home which is more suited to code, post processed results, reports etc).

The hpc-work directories provide free, 1TB storage for the personal use of Cambridge researchers. This storage cannot be shared with other users - please purchase shared storage from the [Research Data Store (RDS)](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store). The hpc-work directories themselves are also provided by the RDS service. National Facility (DiRAC/Tier2/IRIS) users may already have additional project areas visible under /rds/user/<username> and ~/rds provided from pre-paid RDS storage which are shared between members of the same project.

Please note that for general users we currently provide two tiers only of non-transient storage (i.e. storage not limited to the runtime of a single job), namely */home* and *hpc-work* (the latter possibly supplemented by additional RDS storage areas if purchased). As stated above RDS directories are based on Lustre and are the correct targets for job I/O. The term *scratch* space if encountered should be understood to refer to these Lustre storage areas.

## Disk quotas[¶](#disk-quotas "Permalink to this headline")

Each user has a limit on the amount of file storage he/she can use in the home directory, via quotas. Quotas on /home are currently set at 50 GB per user. Use the command quota to list your current usage - this reports for /home, /rds/user/<username>/hpc-work and any RDS projects you may have. A \* symbol next

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Python Environment Management / Package Installation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to install Dedalus while relying on the cluster-wide conda module. The supported path is to install a personal Miniconda/Miniforge instance in `hpc-work`, then follow the upstream installation method from that personal environment. The user does not need to manually choose cluster MPI/FFTW module versions if they install the package through the documented conda-forge workflow.

ACTION
1) Do not use the shared cluster conda module for this workflow.
2) Install Miniconda or Miniforge in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Create the environment there and follow the upstream Dedalus installation instructions.
4) Once installed, use Slurm or an interactive job as normal to run the code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Guillaume,

Please avoid using the cluster’s shared conda module for this install. Instead, install your own Miniconda or Miniforge under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create the Dedalus environment there.

If you follow the Dedalus installation guidance from that personal environment, you should not need to manually select Intel MPI/FFTW module versions for the basic conda-forge install path.

Once the environment is ready, you can run the code via Slurm as usual, or use an interactive job if you want to test it first.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Prefer user-managed conda installs in `hpc-work` over the outdated shared conda module.

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Installation / HPC
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to install the Dedalus package [1] on CSD3 and run it on multiple cores. The installation requires loading specific modules, setting environment variables, and activating a virtual environment [2]. The user has tried installing using conda and pip, but is experiencing issues with running the package in parallel [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To install Dedalus, follow the instructions in [2] and [4]. Load the required modules, set the environment variables, and activate the virtual environment. To run Dedalus in parallel, use the `salloc` command with the correct options, such as `salloc -p skylake -N1 -n8 -t 1:0:0 --qos INTR` [3]. Make sure to specify the correct number of tasks and nodes.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the error messages you are seeing when running Dedalus in parallel?
- Have you checked the documentation for Dedalus and CSD3 for any specific requirements or recommendations for running the package in parallel?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
To install and run Dedalus on CSD3, please follow these steps:
1. Load the required modules: `module load bzip2-1.0.6-intel-17.0.4-bub5tbg`, `module load ncurses-6.0-intel-17.0.4-tuavzmv`, etc. [2].
2. Set the environment variables: `export FFTW_PATH=/usr/local/software/spack/spack-0.11.2/opt/spack/linux-rhel7-x86_64/intel-17.0.4/fftw-3.3.6-pl2-45kexpwgmgvqnuq4yse2vjee3hbvxnyb`, etc. [2].
3. Activate the virtual environment: `source /home/lc765/venv-dedalus/bin/activate` [2].
4. Run Dedalus using the `salloc` command: `salloc -p skylake -N1 -n8 -t 1:0:0 --qos INTR` [3].
If you encounter any issues, please provide the error messages and we will be happy to help.

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Make sure to load the correct modules and set the environment variables to avoid any conflicts or issues [2].
- Be aware of the queue priorities and usage policies on CSD3 to ensure fair usage of resources [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/sapphire.html::chunk::0001
[2] : HPCSSUP-15213
[3] : https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/docs/mpi-library/developer-guide-linux/2021-16/run-the-application-with-a-container.html::chunk::0001
[4] : HPCSSUP-26983

Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an
[interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids) and work on a sapphire partition node

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi, 
I am a researcher at DAMTP, username lc765. 
I would like to install the code Dedalus ([https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#installing-the-dedalus-package](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#installing-the-dedalus-package)
) on the cluster. 
I am in John Taylor's group.  
  
Dedalus is a flexible (PDE)-solver software that takes the form of a Python 3 package that includes custom C-extensions (compiled with Cython) and that relies on MPI, FFTW (linked
to MPI), HDF5, and a basic scientific-Python stack: numpy, scipy, mpi4py (linked to the same MPI), and h5py.
I can build a stand-alone installation but I'd prefer to instead use the modules available on CSD3. 
I was wondering whether Dedalus has been installed for other users and/or if you have some tips on how I should proceed (e.g. is there a virtual environment with all relevant modules I could simply activate?). 
Thank you very much, 
Best 
Louis Couston

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Installing Package

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Conor,
Please try to install additional software in your own account yourself. You can install dedalus by following the conda instructions at 
[https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures)
which boils down to this:
```
mkdir ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
cd ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/DedalusProject/dedalus_conda/master/install_conda.sh
# Edit install_conda.sh:
BLAS="mkl"
PYTHON_VERSION="3.7"
# - end edits

module load miniconda/3
conda activate base
bash install_conda.sh
```
This will (at length) create a dedalus conda environment which you can then use by doing
conda activate dedalus
then running
python3 -m dedalus command
but please note the test, bench and cov functions don't work because of some problem in the python itself - some dependency issue not handled correctly by conda. If this method of installation doesn't in fact produce a functioning dedalus then please try one of the other installation methods described on the above web page, but please bear in mind that we can't support every item of 3rd party software that one can download from the internet.
In order to run this thing inside a job script then remember to perform the
module load miniconda/3
conda activate dedalus
commands in your job script.
Best regards,
Stuart

---
 
From: Christoforos Hadjigeorgiou <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 30 January 2019 12:28:00
To: Couston, Louis-Alexandre J.H.
Subject: HPCSSUP-15213 installing the DEDALUS code on CSD3 Skylake nodes 
   
—-—-—-—
Reply above this line.
Christoforos Hadjigeorgiou commented:
Hi Louis,
For MPI and compilers those are already loaded as part of the default environment.
For HDF5 and FFTW you will also need to load and set some environment variables:
module load fftw-3.3.6-pl2-intel-17.0.4-45kexpw
module load hdf5-1.10.1-intel-17.0.4-acprvzg
export FFTW_PATH=/usr/local/software/spack/spack-0.11.2/opt/spack/linux-rhel7-x86_64/intel-17.0.4/fftw-3.3.6-pl2-45kexpwgmgvqnuq4yse2vjee3hbvxnyb
export FFTW_LIBRARY_PATH=$FFTW_PATH/lib
export FFTW_INCLUDE_PATH=$FFTW_PATH/include
export MPI_PATH=/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/intel/2017.4/compilers_and_libraries_2017.4.196/linux/mpi/intel64
export MPI_LIBRARY_PATH=$MPI_PATH/lib
export MPI_INCLUDE_PATH=$MPI_PATH/include
Some environment variables may be unnecessary but I set them to avoid any complains during install.
To install dedalus using pip in the virtual environment:
pip install dedalus
To install dedalus manually I ran while inside the venv:
hg clone [https://bitbucket.org/dedalus-project/dedaluscd](https://bitbucket.org/dedalus-project/dedaluscd) dedalus
pip install -r ./requirements
pip install .
1. These two steps may be unnecessary, you can try running dedalus without them first
 python3 ./setup.py build_ext --inplace 
 python3 ./setup.py install
 
To run dedalus for both pip and manual installation I did:
LD_PRELOAD=$MPI_PATH/lib/libmpi.so python3
Without the preload FFTW complains about not finding MPI functions.
Let us know how it goes.
Kind regards,

retrieved_contexts:
Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.peta4-icelake
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs using icelake, but can be modified for
other types of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts,
please see the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs not using MPI[¶](#jobs-not-using-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

In this case you should be able to simply specify the sapphire partition to
the -p sbatch directive, e.g.:

```
#SBATCH -p sapphire
```

will submit a job able to run on the first nodes available in the
sapphire partition.
If you need more than 4580 MiB of memory, you will need to specify it as a sbatch directive like this:

```
#SBATCH --mem=8000
```

to ask for 8000 MiB memory.

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using *Intel MPI 2021.6.0* on Sapphire Rapid nodes. There are
other, related changes to the default environment seen by jobs running
on the sapphire nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against the
Sapphire Rapid environment, the simplest option is to use a login node as shown above. Alternatively, request an
[interactive node](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids) and work on a sapphire partition node

[SUMMARY] installing the DEDALUS code on CSD3 Skylake nodes

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hi, 
I am a researcher at DAMTP, username lc765. 
I would like to install the code Dedalus ([https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#installing-the-dedalus-package](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#installing-the-dedalus-package)
) on the cluster. 
I am in John Taylor's group.  
  
Dedalus is a flexible (PDE)-solver software that takes the form of a Python 3 package that includes custom C-extensions (compiled with Cython) and that relies on MPI, FFTW (linked
to MPI), HDF5, and a basic scientific-Python stack: numpy, scipy, mpi4py (linked to the same MPI), and h5py.
I can build a stand-alone installation but I'd prefer to instead use the modules available on CSD3. 
I was wondering whether Dedalus has been installed for other users and/or if you have some tips on how I should proceed (e.g. is there a virtual environment with all relevant modules I could simply activate?). 
Thank you very much, 
Best 
Louis Couston

[SUMMARY] Installing Package

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
Could you please install the following on my account (ch782):
[https://github.com/DedalusProject/dedalus](https://github.com/DedalusProject/dedalus)
Thank You,
Best Regards,
Conor Heffernan

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Dear Conor,
Please try to install additional software in your own account yourself. You can install dedalus by following the conda instructions at 
[https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures](https://dedalus-project.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#alternative-installation-procedures)
which boils down to this:
```
mkdir ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
cd ~/rds/hpc-work/dedalus
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/DedalusProject/dedalus_conda/master/install_conda.sh
# Edit install_conda.sh:
BLAS="mkl"
PYTHON_VERSION="3.7"
# - end edits

module load miniconda/3
conda activate base
bash install_conda.sh
```
This will (at length) create a dedalus conda environment which you can then use by doing
conda activate dedalus
then running
python3 -m dedalus command
but please note the test, bench and cov functions don't work because of some problem in the python itself - some dependency issue not handled correctly by conda. If this method of installation doesn't in fact produce a functioning dedalus then please try one of the other installation methods described on the above web page, but please bear in mind that we can't support every item of 3rd party software that one can download from the internet.
In order to run this thing inside a job script then remember to perform the
module load miniconda/3
conda activate dedalus
commands in your job script.
Best regards,
Stuart
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Stuart,
I attempted to install the software but when I tried 'conda activate 
base' the following error occured:
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 
'conda activate'.
If your shell is Bash or a Bourne variant, enable conda for the current 
user with
$ echo ". 
/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/miniconda3/4.5.1/etc/profile.d/conda.sh" >> 
~/.bashrc
or, for all users, enable conda with
$ sudo ln -s 
/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/miniconda3/4.5.1/etc/profile.d/conda.sh 
/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
The options above will permanently enable the 'conda' command, but they 
do NOT
put conda's base (root) environment on PATH.  To do so, run
$ conda activate
in your terminal, or to put the base environment on PATH permanently, 
run
$ echo "conda activate" >> ~/.bashrc
Previous to conda 4.4, the recommended way to activate conda was to 
modify PATH in
your ~/.bashrc file.  You should manually remove the line that looks 
like
export PATH="/usr/local/Cluster-Apps/miniconda3/4.5.1/bin:$PATH"
^^^ The above line should NO LONGER be in your ~/.bashrc file! ^^^
Running the above suggestions didn't resolve the problem and just kept 
regenerating the above error. I tried the other installation methods but 
it seems to have a problem with user permission to install the Cython 
library. Any help resolving this issue would be appreciated.
Best Regards,
Conor

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Version Support / Licensing
Technical Action Required: No (policy clarification)
Escalation Required: Yes (new build request if required)
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The immediate answer is that `abaqus/2024` is not currently available as a centrally supported version on the cluster; the latest supported central version is `abaqus/2022`. If there is a requirement for a newer version, licensing and a separate build or install request would need to be coordinated.

ACTION
1) Confirm that `abaqus/2022` is the latest supported central version.
2) Explain that no timeline can be promised for `abaqus/2024`.
3) If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, ask them to submit a fresh build or install request to HPC support.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Zac,

At present the newest centrally supported version on the cluster is `abaqus/2022`, so the user did not misunderstand.

If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, please send a new request to `support@hpc.cam.ac.uk` and we can look into building and providing that version.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

Category: Software / Abaqus Compatibility
Technical Action Required: Yes
Portal Action Required: No

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is inquiring about using Abaqus 2024 on the HPC cluster, and there is evidence that the version may not be compatible with the cluster's current setup [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
To confirm compatibility, we need to verify the version of Abaqus available on the cluster. The user should check the official documentation for available software versions [1]. If Abaqus 2024 is not available, the user may need to discuss alternative options with their PI or the Engineering team [3].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What is the exact error message or issue encountered when trying to use Abaqus 2024 on the cluster?
- Has the user requested access to the Abaqus license server and been approved [3]?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for your inquiry about using Abaqus 2024 on the HPC cluster. We are currently investigating the compatibility of this version with our cluster. In the meantime, please check the official documentation for available software versions [1]. If you have any further questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to reach out.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- When using licensed software, it is essential to follow the official documentation and guidelines to ensure compatibility and access [1][3].
- Users should always prioritize official documentation and support channels when troubleshooting issues [4].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/getting_support.html::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-91919
[4] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/getting_support.html::chunk::0002

3) If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, ask them to submit a fresh build or install request to HPC support.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

If the relevant licence owner wants a newer centrally available version, please send a new request to `support@hpc.cam.ac.uk` and we can look into building and providing that version.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:**

4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical

Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your

[TICKET_KEY] HPCSSUP-91919
[STATUS] RESOLVED
[CREATED] 2025-08-26T13:25:20.380+0100
[SUMMARY] Web-interface job submission

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Hello,
I am trying to submit a job onto the cluster and I have the following questions (this is my first time using this):
1.
On the job composer on the create new job tab, I selected the default template and it looks like the below. I want to submit an Abaqus job, which uses an inp. File. To include this into the job submission do I just have to select the edit file option and upload my file without having to adjust the script of the default template. I am not sure how to proceed
[cid:21b1bc74-b137-411b-b0d2-8919700ac1c5]
Kind regards,
Umara

[CONVERSATION]
<MESSAGE id=0001 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
</MESSAGE>

<MESSAGE id=0002 role=HELPDESK_ASSIGNEE>
Hi Umara,
Can you confirm please if you’ve accessed our docs page on the job composer? https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#job-management 
In any case, go ahead and scroll to the bottom of the job composer then hit this button
[FILE ATTACHMENT]
Which should let you add the path to the `.inp` file  with something like 
```
# Define variables
MODEL_NAME="yourname"
STUDY_NAME="yourname"
JOB_NAME="yourname"
INPUT_DIR="path/to/${MODEL_NAME}/${STUDY_NAME}"
```
Then add: 
```
input=${STUDY_NAME}.in
```
to the launch options
Best wishes, 
Henry
HPC Support

<MESSAGE id=0003 role=TICKET_CREATOR>
Dear Henry,
Thank you for the response, I am quite confused as I have read the documentation, but it does not go through the script editing, which is what I am unfamiliar with.
So, for the excerpt that you have shown, do I just copy this at the end of the script? So, the Model and study name is just the name of my inp file and the job name is what I called this job. The directory is the directory that contains the template script with my inp. File uploaded into the same folder, or just the location of my inp. file?
I have edited the script like this and was wondering where I would insert the section you mentioned above:
#!/bin/bash
#!
#! Example SLURM job script for Peta4-Skylake (Skylake CPUs, OPA)
#! Last updated: Mon 13 Nov 12:25:17 GMT 2017
#!
#!#############################################################
#!#### Modify the options in this section as appropriate ######
#!#############################################################
#! sbatch directives begin here ###############################
#! Name of the job:
SBATCH -J Index_THV
#! Which project should be charged:
#SBATCH -A CHANGEME
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
SBATCH --nodes=2
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total? (<= nodes*32)
#! The skylake/skylake-himem nodes have 32 CPUs (cores) each.
SBATCH --ntasks=64
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
SBATCH --mail-type=FAIL
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
SBATCH --no-requeue
#! For 6GB per CPU, set "-p skylake"; for 12GB per CPU, set "-p skylake-himem":
#SBATCH -p skylake
#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)
#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by core number*walltime.
#! The --ntasks value refers to the number of tasks to be launched by SLURM only. This
#! usually equates to the number of MPI tasks launched. Reduce this from nodes*32 if
#! demanded by memory requirements, or if OMP_NUM_THREADS>1.
#! Each task is allocated 1 core by default, and each core is allocated 5980MB (skylake)
#! and 12030MB (skylake-himem). If this is insufficient, also specify
#! --cpus-per-task and/or --mem (the latter specifies MB per node).
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES

### What Can I Do?[¶](#what-can-i-do "Permalink to this headline")

The following items should help offer some clarity on how to engage with the CSD3/Dawn support team. Please consult and follow this advice.

1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.
2. **Be explicit:** use a descriptive subject line. In the body of your email, provide as much relevant information as possible and avoid pasting errors or log files without context. Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.
4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical

Rapids HBM[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-sapphire-rapids-hbm "Permalink to this headline")

Since the cpu-r-hbm nodes are running Rocky 9, you will want to recompile
your applications if they are coming from a different operating system or a different OS version.

It is possible to log into a login node with compatible OS/software by logging into *login-sapphire.hpc.cam.ac.uk*,
which will land you on one of the *login-s-\** nodes.

Alternatively, request an interactive node using *sintr*:

```
sintr -t 1:0:0 -N1 -n38 -A YOURPROJECT-CPU -p ukaea-spr-hbm(-flat) -q INTR
```

to rebuild and re-optimise your applications to get the best from these specialist nodes. The `-q INTR` option used above requests a higher priority interactive jobs, but does not allow for more than 1h wall time. If you need a longer interactive session, you will need to remove the option `-q INTR` from your `sintr` command line and queue for longer.

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2 and SL3
respectively.

The per-job, per-user cpu limits are now 4256 and 448 cpus for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Sapphire Rapids[¶](#default-submission-script-for-sapphire-rapids "Permalink to this headline")

There is no default job submission template dedicated to Sapphire Rapid nodes, but you might be able to make one for yourself by tweaking an Icelake template.

You should find a symbolic link to an Icelake default job submission script modified for the icelake nodes in your

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: SRCP / Package Deployment / R Module
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
This is an urgent request to make the SNPolisher package available on a specific SRCP platform. The correct initial support path is to prioritise escalation to the SRCP/platform team for package installation. Once availability is confirmed, the next step is to validate the package in a plain R session before troubleshooting any surrounding workflow/tool integration.

ACTION
1) Escalate the request to the SRCP/platform team as a priority package-installation request.
2) Once the package is reported available, validate it in a plain R session.
3) If the user's external tool still fails after installation, identify the tool name and R path/environment it uses.
4) Update the user once installation status has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Justin,

We are treating this as a priority request and are arranging installation of SNPolisher with the SRCP/platform team.

Once the package is available, we will send you the relevant load instructions and, if needed, follow up with checks to make sure your workflow is using the correct R environment.

Best regards,
HPC Support

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the platform ID and the required R package version with the user [1][7].

3. Perform a risk assessment for the package installation, if necessary [1][4][7].
4. Obtain approval from the platform manager for the package installation [1][4][7].
5. Install the R package on the SRCP platform using the approved method [2][3][6].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)

- Have you checked if the package is already installed on the platform or if a similar request has been made before?

Best regards,

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler

# R[¶](#r "Link to this heading")

Like Python, R has its own built-in build system.

The R build system is remarkably uniform and well-tested.
This makes it one of the easiest build systems to create new Spack packages for.

## Phases[¶](#phases "Link to this heading")

The `RBuilder` and `RPackage` base classes have a single phase:

1. `install` - install the package

By default, this phase runs the following command:

```
$ R CMD INSTALL --library=/path/to/installation/prefix/rlib/R/library .
```

## Finding R packages[¶](#finding-r-packages "Link to this heading")

The vast majority of R packages are hosted on CRAN - The Comprehensive R Archive Network.
If you are looking for a particular R package, search for “CRAN <package-name>” and you should quickly find what you want.
If it isn’t on CRAN, try Bioconductor, another common R repository.

For the purposes of this tutorial, we will be walking through [r-caret](https://github.com/spack/spack-packages/blob/develop/repos/spack_repo/builtin/packages/r_caret/package.py) as an example.
If you search for “CRAN caret”, you will quickly find what you are looking for at [https://cran.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cran.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret). <https://cran.r-project.org> is the main CRAN website.
However, CRAN also has a <https://cloud.r-project.org> site that automatically redirects to [mirrors around the world](https://cloud.r-project.org/mirrors.html).
For stability and performance reasons, we will use [https://cloud.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cloud.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret).
If you search for “Package source”, you will find the download URL for the latest release.
Use this URL with `spack create` to create a new package.

## Package name[¶](#package-name "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll notice is that Spack prepends `r-` to the front of the package name.
This is how Spack separates R extensions from the rest of the packages in Spack.
Without this, we would end up with package name collisions more frequently than we would like.
For instance, there are already packages for both:

* `ape` and `r-ape`
* `curl` and `r-curl`
* `gmp` and `r-gmp`
* `jpeg` and `r-jpeg`
* `openssl` and `r-openssl`
* `uuid` and `r-uuid`
* `xts` and `r-xts`

Many popular programs written in C/C++ are later ported to R as a separate project.

## Description[¶](#description "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll need to add to your new package is a description.
The top of the homepage for `caret` lists the following description:

> Classification and Regression Training
>
> Misc functions for training and plotting classification and regression models.

The first line is a short

source activate openmm-env/
python -m openmm.testInstallation # update with your own openmm python script. This runs the default tests.
conda deactivate
```

Here, we are using OpenMM’s own testing suite to ensure the application is running properly.
The output should be similar to the following (times might be slightly different):

```

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler

# R[¶](#r "Link to this heading")

Like Python, R has its own built-in build system.

The R build system is remarkably uniform and well-tested.
This makes it one of the easiest build systems to create new Spack packages for.

## Phases[¶](#phases "Link to this heading")

The `RBuilder` and `RPackage` base classes have a single phase:

1. `install` - install the package

By default, this phase runs the following command:

```
$ R CMD INSTALL --library=/path/to/installation/prefix/rlib/R/library .
```

## Finding R packages[¶](#finding-r-packages "Link to this heading")

The vast majority of R packages are hosted on CRAN - The Comprehensive R Archive Network.
If you are looking for a particular R package, search for “CRAN <package-name>” and you should quickly find what you want.
If it isn’t on CRAN, try Bioconductor, another common R repository.

For the purposes of this tutorial, we will be walking through [r-caret](https://github.com/spack/spack-packages/blob/develop/repos/spack_repo/builtin/packages/r_caret/package.py) as an example.
If you search for “CRAN caret”, you will quickly find what you are looking for at [https://cran.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cran.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret). <https://cran.r-project.org> is the main CRAN website.
However, CRAN also has a <https://cloud.r-project.org> site that automatically redirects to [mirrors around the world](https://cloud.r-project.org/mirrors.html).
For stability and performance reasons, we will use [https://cloud.r-project.org/package=caret](https://cloud.r-project.org/package%3Dcaret).
If you search for “Package source”, you will find the download URL for the latest release.
Use this URL with `spack create` to create a new package.

## Package name[¶](#package-name "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll notice is that Spack prepends `r-` to the front of the package name.
This is how Spack separates R extensions from the rest of the packages in Spack.
Without this, we would end up with package name collisions more frequently than we would like.
For instance, there are already packages for both:

* `ape` and `r-ape`
* `curl` and `r-curl`
* `gmp` and `r-gmp`
* `jpeg` and `r-jpeg`
* `openssl` and `r-openssl`
* `uuid` and `r-uuid`
* `xts` and `r-xts`

Many popular programs written in C/C++ are later ported to R as a separate project.

## Description[¶](#description "Link to this heading")

The first thing you’ll need to add to your new package is a description.
The top of the homepage for `caret` lists the following description:

> Classification and Regression Training
>
> Misc functions for training and plotting classification and regression models.

The first line is a short

condition.preset_description: Frozen validity-aware reranker over local docs, external docs, and tickets.

metrics.reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Runtime Failure / Node Health / Slurm Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The job is intermittently failing on a specific GPU node with CUDA device availability errors. Two grounded mitigations from the submitted command/output are to ensure the correct Ampere base module stack is loaded and to let Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm instead of forcing `-npernode` and `-np`.

ACTION
1) Load the correct base stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/default-amp`
2) Remove manual `mpirun -npernode ... -np ...` overrides where possible.
3) Prefer `--tasks-per-node=4` rather than only `--ntasks=8` for the reported two-node case.
4) As a temporary workaround, route around the bad node with:
   `#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`
5) If the issue persists after these changes or starts affecting other nodes, escalate for node-health investigation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Dom,

Please first make sure your script loads `rhel8/default-amp`, as that adds the expected CUDA runtime environment.

We also recommend removing the explicit `-npernode`/`-np` arguments and letting Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm. For a two-node, eight-task job, `--tasks-per-node=4` is the safer Slurm-side request.

In the short term, you can avoid the problematic node with:
`#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`

Best wishes,
HPC Support
metrics.response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Job Submission / Resource Allocation [1][5]
Technical Action Required: Yes [1]
Escalation Required: Possibly [8]
Urgency: Medium [1]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with job submissions on the Ampere nodes, specifically encountering a "CUDA error" indicating that all CUDA-capable devices are busy or unavailable [4]. This issue seems to be related to the allocation of GPUs and the configuration of the job submission script [1][5]. The user has provided an example slurm.out file, which may help in diagnosing the issue [4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the job submission script to ensure that the correct number of GPUs is requested and that the script is configured correctly for the Ampere nodes [1][5].
2. Check the status of the nodes and the availability of GPUs using the `squeue` and `sinfo` commands [5].
3. Consider adding the `--gres=gpu:<number>` directive to the job submission script to specify the number of GPUs required [1][6].
4. If the issue persists, try rebooting the node or contacting the HPC support team for further assistance [4][8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the job submission script and the specific error message encountered?
- Have you tried submitting the job with a different number of GPUs or on a different node?
- Are there any other jobs or processes running on the node that could be causing the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with your job submission on the Ampere nodes. We will review the job submission script and the node status to diagnose the issue. In the meantime, please try adding the `--gres=gpu:<number>` directive to your script and resubmit the job. If the issue persists, please contact us for further assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always ensure that the correct number of GPUs is requested and that the job submission script is configured correctly for the Ampere nodes [1][5].
- Be aware of the node status and GPU availability before submitting jobs [5].
- Follow the guidelines for job submission and resource allocation on the HPC system [1][5].

[4] : HPCSSUP-100531

[8] : HPCSSUP-86140

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: GPU Runtime Failure / Node Health / Slurm Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The job is intermittently failing on a specific GPU node with CUDA device availability errors. Two grounded mitigations from the submitted command/output are to ensure the correct Ampere base module stack is loaded and to let Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm instead of forcing `-npernode` and `-np`.

ACTION
1) Load the correct base stack:
   `module purge`
   `module load rhel8/default-amp`
2) Remove manual `mpirun -npernode ... -np ...` overrides where possible.
3) Prefer `--tasks-per-node=4` rather than only `--ntasks=8` for the reported two-node case.
4) As a temporary workaround, route around the bad node with:
   `#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`
5) If the issue persists after these changes or starts affecting other nodes, escalate for node-health investigation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Dom,

Please first make sure your script loads `rhel8/default-amp`, as that adds the expected CUDA runtime environment.

We also recommend removing the explicit `-npernode`/`-np` arguments and letting Open MPI infer task placement from Slurm. For a two-node, eight-task job, `--tasks-per-node=4` is the safer Slurm-side request.

In the short term, you can avoid the problematic node with:
`#SBATCH --exclude=gpu-q-29`

Best wishes,
HPC Support

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with job submissions on the Ampere nodes, specifically encountering a "CUDA error" indicating that all CUDA-capable devices are busy or unavailable [4]. This issue seems to be related to the allocation of GPUs and the configuration of the job submission script [1][5]. The user has provided an example slurm.out file, which may help in diagnosing the issue [4].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Review the job submission script to ensure that the correct number of GPUs is requested and that the script is configured correctly for the Ampere nodes [1][5].
2. Check the status of the nodes and the availability of GPUs using the `squeue` and `sinfo` commands [5].
3. Consider adding the `--gres=gpu:<number>` directive to the job submission script to specify the number of GPUs required [1][6].
4. If the issue persists, try rebooting the node or contacting the HPC support team for further assistance [4][8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide more details about the job submission script and the specific error message encountered?
- Have you tried submitting the job with a different number of GPUs or on a different node?
- Are there any other jobs or processes running on the node that could be causing the issue?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,
Thank you for reporting the issue with your job submission on the Ampere nodes. We will review the job submission script and the node status to diagnose the issue. In the meantime, please try adding the `--gres=gpu:<number>` directive to your script and resubmit the job. If the issue persists, please contact us for further assistance.
Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Always ensure that the correct number of GPUs is requested and that the job submission script is configured correctly for the Ampere nodes [1][5].
- Be aware of the node status and GPU availability before submitting jobs [5].
- Follow the guidelines for job submission and resource allocation on the HPC system [1][5].

## Recommendations for running on ampere[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

Since the gpu-q nodes are running Rocky8, you will want to recompile
your applications. We suggest you do this by requesting an
[interactive node](#software).

The per-job wallclock time limits are 36 hours and 12 hours for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

The per-job, per-user GPU limits are currently 64 and 32 GPUs for SL1/2
and SL3 respectively.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for ampere[¶](#default-submission-script-for-ampere "Permalink to this headline")

You should find a symbolic link to an Ampere default job submission script modified for the ampere nodes in your home directory, called:

```
slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

This is set up for non-MPI jobs, but can be modified for other types
of job. If you prefer to modify your existing job scripts, please see
the following sections for guidance.

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p ampere
```

Slurm will enforce allocation of a proportional number of CPUs (32) per GPU.

Note that if you either do not specify a number of GPUs per node with *–gres*, or request more than one node
with less than 4 GPUs per node, you will receive an error on

Just wanted to ask about/report some unusual behaviour we've been seeing sometimes​ when using running python scripts using OpenMM on the Ampere nodes. These jobs sometimes work fine, and sometimes fail with an error saying "No compatible CUDA device is available".
I've been using very similar scripts on CSD3 for a long time and never encountered errors like these before, but they have been popping up in the last few days. An identical script can sometimes work and sometimes not, seemingly at random. It's not really a big problem, as we can just keep submitting until it works which usually only takes 2 or 3 attempts, but it is unusual and not ideal.
I've attached a full output from slurm to show the error.
Thanks, and best wishes,
Kieran Russell

terminated.
The slurmd daemon maintains a list of the jobs which have already been
terminated to avoid replay of task launch requests.
If the slurmctld daemon is cold-started (with the "-c" option
or "/etc/init.d/slurm startclean"), it starts job ID values
over based upon **FirstJobId**.
If the slurmd is not also cold-started, it will reject job launch requests
for jobs that it considers terminated.
This solution to this problem is to cold-start all slurmd daemons whenever
the slurmctld daemon is cold-started.

**"Unable to accept new connection: Too many open
files"**
The srun command automatically increases its open file limit to
the hard limit in order to process all of the standard input and output
connections to the launched tasks. It is recommended that you set the
open file hard limit to 8192 across the cluster.

***SlurmdDebug* fails to log job step information
at the appropriate level**
There are two programs involved here. One is **slurmd**, which is
a persistent daemon running at the desired debug level. The second
program is **slurmstepd**, which executes the user job and its
debug level is controlled by the user. Submitting the job with
an option of *--debug=#* will result in the desired level of
detail being logged in the *SlurmdLogFile* plus the output
of the program.

**"Batch JobId=# missing from batch node <node>
(not found BatchStartTime after startup)"**
A shell is launched on node zero of a job's allocation to execute
the submitted program. The *slurmd* daemon executing on each compute
node will periodically report to the *slurmctld* what programs it
is executing. If a batch program is expected to be running on some
node (i.e. node zero of the job's allocation) and is not found, the
message above will be logged and the job canceled. This typically is
associated with exhausting memory on the node or some other critical
failure that cannot be recovered from.

PMIx uses the **hwloc API** for different purposes, including
*OS device* features like querying sysfs folders (such as
*/sys/class/net* and */sys/class/infiniband*) to get the names of
Infiniband HCAs. With the above mentioned features, hwloc defaults to
querying the OpenCL devices, which creates handles on */dev/nvidia\** files.
These handles are kept by slurmstepd and will result in the following error
inside a job:

```
$ nvidia-smi mig --id 1 --create-gpu-instance FOO,FOO --default-compute-instance
Unable to create a GPU instance on GPU 1 using profile FOO: In use by another client
```

In order to use Multi-Instance GPUs with Slurm and PMIx you can instruct hwloc
to not query OpenCL devices by setting the
HWLOC\_COMPONENTS=-opencl environment
variable for slurmd, i.e. setting this variable in systemd unit file for slurmd.

**"srun: error: Unable to accept connection:
Resources temporarily unavailable"**
This has been reported on some larger clusters running SUSE Linux when
a user's resource limits are reached. You may need to increase limits
for locked memory and stack size to resolve this problem.

**"Warning: Note very large processing time"
in *SlurmctldLogFile***
This error is indicative

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

## Problems[¶](#problems "Permalink to this headline")

Please check first of all whether there is an answer to your question in the FAQ. If not, please request support with your details and

```
AccountingStorageTres=gres/gpu
NodeName=n1 Gres=gpu:a100:2
```

AutoDetect=nvml
```

Here's an example of a job with two tasks that uses one GPU per task, two
GPUs total.

```
$ srun --tres-per-task=gres/gpu:1 -n2 --gpus=2 --mem=2G gpu_burn
GPU 0: NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB (UUID: GPU-...)
GPU 0: NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB (UUID: GPU-...)
```

After the job has finished, we can see utilization details of these TRES
using sacct. Note that there are multiple tasks here and we can use
TRESUsageIn[Min,Max,Ave,Tot] to examine the "highwater marks" for gpumem and
gpuutil over all the ranks in the step, as is true for other TRESUsage\*
values:

```
$ sacct -j 1277.0 --format=tresusageinave -p
TRESUsageInAve|
cpu=00:00:11,energy=0,fs/disk=87613,gres/gpumem=36266M,gres/gpuutil=100,mem=628748K,pages=0,vmem=0|
$ sacct -j 1277.0 --format=tresusageintot -p
TRESUsageInTot|
cpu=00:00:22,energy=0,fs/disk=175227,gres/gpumem=72532M,gres/gpuutil=200,mem=1257496K,pages=0,vmem=0|
```

## GPU Management

In the case of Slurm's GRES plugin for GPUs, the environment variable
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
is set for each job step to determine which GPUs are
available for its use on each node. This environment variable is only set
when tasks are launched on a specific compute node (no global environment
variable is set for the *salloc* command and the environment variable set
for the *sbatch* command only reflects the GPUs allocated to that job
on that node, node zero of the allocation).
CUDA version 3.1 (or higher) uses this environment
variable in order to run multiple jobs or job steps on a node with GPUs
and ensure that the resources assigned to each are unique. In the example
above, the allocated node may have four or more graphics devices. In that
case, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
will reference unique devices for each file and
the output might resemble this:

```
JobStep=1234.0 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1
JobStep=1234.1 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2
JobStep=1234.2 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3
```

**NOTE**: Be sure to specify the *File* parameters in the
*gres.conf* file and ensure they are in the increasing numeric order.

The `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
environment variable will also be set in the job's Prolog and Epilog programs.
Note that the environment variable set for the job may differ from that set for
the Prolog and Epilog if Slurm is configured to constrain the device files
visible to a job using Linux cgroup.
This is because the Prolog and Epilog programs run outside of any Linux
cgroup while the job runs inside of the cgroup and may thus have a
different set of visible devices.
For example, if a job is allocated the device "/dev/nvidia1", then
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a value of
"1" in the Prolog and Epilog while the job's value of
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a
value of "0" (i.e. the first GPU device visible to the job).
For more information see the
[Prolog and Epilog Guide](prolog_epilog.html).

When possible, Slurm automatically determines the GPUs on the system using
NVML. NVML (which powers the
`nvidia-smi` tool) numbers GPUs in order by their
PCI bus IDs. For this numbering to match the numbering reported by CUDA,

partition directive, i.e.

```
#SBATCH -p icelake
```

for 3.4GB per CPU nodes, and

```
#SBATCH -p icelake-himem
```

for 6.8GB per CPU nodes. Jobs requesting more than 3.4GB per CPU may still be submitted to the icelake partition, but may be automatically assigned extra CPUs to cover the additional memory, and will therefore be charged more usage credits. Therefore it is recommended that whenever possible jobs requiring more than 3.4GB per CPU be submitted to icelake-himem.

The cclake and cclake-himem partitions work in similar ways but the exact numbers vary according to the node hardware - see the template job submission scripts for details.

The Wilkes3-GPU (ampere) nodes each contain 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs. It is possible to request 1, 2, 3, or 4 GPUs for a single node job, but multinode jobs will need to request 4 GPUs per node, which would be done by using the directives

```
#SBATCH -p ampere
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

(see `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3`).

The job’s status in the queue can be monitored with *squeue*; alternatively use *qstat* or *showq* (add -u username to focus on a particular user’s jobs).

The job can be deleted with *scancel <job\_id>* or *qdel <job\_id>*.

When the job finishes (in error or correctly) there will normally be one file created in the submission directory with a name of the form slurm-NNNN.out (where NNNN is the job id).

Here's an example of a job with two tasks that uses one GPU per task, two
GPUs total.

```
$ srun --tres-per-task=gres/gpu:1 -n2 --gpus=2 --mem=2G gpu_burn
GPU 0: NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB (UUID: GPU-...)
GPU 0: NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB (UUID: GPU-...)
```

After the job has finished, we can see utilization details of these TRES
using sacct. Note that there are multiple tasks here and we can use
TRESUsageIn[Min,Max,Ave,Tot] to examine the "highwater marks" for gpumem and
gpuutil over all the ranks in the step, as is true for other TRESUsage\*
values:

```
$ sacct -j 1277.0 --format=tresusageinave -p
TRESUsageInAve|
cpu=00:00:11,energy=0,fs/disk=87613,gres/gpumem=36266M,gres/gpuutil=100,mem=628748K,pages=0,vmem=0|
$ sacct -j 1277.0 --format=tresusageintot -p
TRESUsageInTot|
cpu=00:00:22,energy=0,fs/disk=175227,gres/gpumem=72532M,gres/gpuutil=200,mem=1257496K,pages=0,vmem=0|
```

## GPU Management

In the case of Slurm's GRES plugin for GPUs, the environment variable
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
is set for each job step to determine which GPUs are
available for its use on each node. This environment variable is only set
when tasks are launched on a specific compute node (no global environment
variable is set for the *salloc* command and the environment variable set
for the *sbatch* command only reflects the GPUs allocated to that job
on that node, node zero of the allocation).
CUDA version 3.1 (or higher) uses this environment
variable in order to run multiple jobs or job steps on a node with GPUs
and ensure that the resources assigned to each are unique. In the example
above, the allocated node may have four or more graphics devices. In that
case, `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
will reference unique devices for each file and
the output might resemble this:

```
JobStep=1234.0 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1
JobStep=1234.1 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2
JobStep=1234.2 CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3
```

**NOTE**: Be sure to specify the *File* parameters in the
*gres.conf* file and ensure they are in the increasing numeric order.

The `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES`
environment variable will also be set in the job's Prolog and Epilog programs.
Note that the environment variable set for the job may differ from that set for
the Prolog and Epilog if Slurm is configured to constrain the device files
visible to a job using Linux cgroup.
This is because the Prolog and Epilog programs run outside of any Linux
cgroup while the job runs inside of the cgroup and may thus have a
different set of visible devices.
For example, if a job is allocated the device "/dev/nvidia1", then
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a value of
"1" in the Prolog and Epilog while the job's value of
`CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` will be set to a
value of "0" (i.e. the first GPU device visible to the job).
For more information see the
[Prolog and Epilog Guide](prolog_epilog.html).

When possible, Slurm automatically determines the GPUs on the system using
NVML. NVML (which powers the
`nvidia-smi` tool) numbers GPUs in order by their
PCI bus IDs. For this numbering to match the numbering reported by CUDA,

Here is the exact sbatch script that I use:
Best wishes,
Kajetan
#!/bin/bash
#!
#! Example SLURM job script for Wilkes3 (AMD EPYC 7763, ConnectX-6, A100)
#! Last updated: Fri 30 Jul 11:07:58 BST 2021
#!
#!#############################################################
#!#### Modify the options in this section as appropriate ######
#!#############################################################
#! sbatch directives begin here ###############################
#! Name of the job:
#SBATCH -J lora_zh
#! Which project should be charged (NB Wilkes2 projects end in '-GPU'):
#SBATCH -A VULIC-SL3-GPU
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#! How many (MPI) tasks will there be in total?
#! Note probably this should not exceed the total number of GPUs in use.
#SBATCH --ntasks=1
#! Specify the number of GPUs per node (between 1 and 4; must be 4 if nodes>1).
#! Note that the job submission script will enforce no more than 32 cpus per GPU.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
#! How much wallclock time will be required?
#SBATCH --time=12:00:00
#! What types of email messages do you wish to receive?
#SBATCH --mail-type=NONE
#! Uncomment this to prevent the job from being requeued (e.g. if
#! interrupted by node failure or system downtime):
##SBATCH --no-requeue
#! Do not change:
#SBATCH -p ampere
#! sbatch directives end here (put any additional directives above this line)
#! Notes:
#! Charging is determined by GPU number*walltime.
#! Number of nodes and tasks per node allocated by SLURM (do not change):
numnodes=$SLURM_JOB_NUM_NODES
numtasks=$SLURM_NTASKS
mpi_tasks_per_node=$(echo "$SLURM_TASKS_PER_NODE" | sed -e 's/^([0-9][0-9]).$/\1/')
#! ############################################################
#! Modify the settings below to specify the application's environment, location
#! and launch method:
#! Optionally modify the environment seen by the application
#! (note that SLURM reproduces the environment at submission irrespective of ~/.bashrc):
. /etc/profile.d/modules.sh # Leave this line (enables the module command)
module purge # Removes all modules still loaded
module load rhel8/default-amp # REQUIRED - loads the basic environment
#! Insert additional module load commands after this line if needed:
source ../venv/bin/activate
#! Full path to application executable:
application="bash run_finetuning.bash"
#! Run options for the application:
options="--language zh"
#! Work directory (i.e. where the job will run):
workdir="$SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR" # The value of SLURM_SUBMIT_DIR sets workdir to the directory
1. in which sbatch is run.
#! Are you using OpenMP (NB this is unrelated to OpenMPI)? If so increase this
#! safe value to no more than 128:
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=1
#! Number of MPI tasks to be started by the application per node and in total (do not change):
np=$[${numnodes}*${mpi_tasks_per_node}]
#! Choose this for a pure shared-memory OpenMP parallel program on a single node:
#! (OMP_NUM_THREADS threads will be created):
CMD="$application $options"
#! Choose this for a MPI code using OpenMPI:
#CMD="mpirun -npernode $mpi_tasks_per_node -np $np $application $options"
###############################################################
1. 1. 1. You should not have to change anything below this line ####
###############################################################
cd $workdir
echo -e "Changed directory to `pwd`.\n"
JOBID=$SLURM_JOB_ID
echo -e "JobID: $JOBID\n======"

ech

CLASSIFICATION
Category: SL2 Credit Provisioning / Procurement Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The requester has sent a PO for SL2 CPU hours to be credited for Emily Lines. From the initial message alone, it is not clear whether these hours should go onto an existing SL2 project or whether a new project account is needed. The correct next step is to confirm the target SL2 account and, if none exists, follow the normal project-creation and approval path.

ACTION
1) Ask whether the credits are for an existing SL2 project or a new one.
2) If no suitable existing SL2 CPU project exists, follow the normal approval process to create one.
3) Add the required users to the target project once the account path is confirmed.
4) If an existing account is intended instead, request the exact target project code.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Anne,

Before we can apply the purchased CPU hours, we need to confirm whether they are for an existing SL2 CPU project or whether a new project needs to be created.

If there is no suitable existing SL2 CPU project for this work, we would need to follow the normal approval process before creating one and applying the hours.

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Are there any specific project or subproject names that these hours should be assigned to?

Best regards,
HPC Support

- Ensure that all purchase orders are verified and valid before processing [3].
- Follow established procedures for adding compute hours to user accounts [3][5].

develop a core in-house knowledge base in HPC systems and scientific support which will be available to all research staff within the University.

The service allows a range of service levels with different features. These are associated with different quality of service definitions within the scheduler, enforcing different job priorities, maximum job sizes and maximum run times.

A service level is attached to a project, which is a group of users led by a principal investigator (PI) who controls (or who can apply for) a line of funding for HPC.

## Service Levels[¶](#service-levels "Permalink to this headline")

The current Service Levels (SLs), which came into operation in November 2017 and which apply to the Peta4 and Wilkes3 clusters, are described below.

Funding units are in the form of usage credits. What these credits represent depends on the type of cluster. For the CPU cluster (Peta4) 1 credit = 1 CPU core for 1 hour. For the GPU cluster (Wilkes3) 1 credit = 1 GPU for 1 hour.

Accounting periods or quarters are three month periods of the year running 1st February - 30th April, 1st May - 31st July, 1st August - 31st October and 1st November - 31st January.

## Service Level 1 – Guaranteed Usage[¶](#service-level-1-guaranteed-usage "Permalink to this headline")

Service Level 1 (SL1) operates with the highest quality of service QOS1, designed for groups which require large and steady amounts of computer time over a long period.

Funds paid will be converted into core hour credits. These credits will be divided over an agreed time period and allocated to quarterly
